risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "After baseline measurements and randomization to either 1,200 or 2,400 mg of NAC per day" and "In this randomized open label pilot study" Comment: The study states that participants were randomized, but it does not describe the random sequence generation method (e.g., computer-generated list, random number table, blocks, or other explicit random process). Under this domain, "randomized" alone without further detail is insufficient to confirm a truly random allocation sequence.

Summary: The paper states that participants were randomized to 1,200 or 2,400 mg NAC, but it gives no description of how the assignment was concealed from the recruiter/enroller. It also describes the study as an “open label pilot study,” which does not establish allocation concealment and may allow foreknowledge, but there is no explicit concealment mechanism reported. Comment: Because no central allocation, sealed opaque sequentially numbered envelopes, identical containers, or other concealment method is explicitly described, allocation concealment must be judged unclear.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "In this randomized open label pilot study, the effects of oral NAC treatment on markers of oxidative stress and hemolysis in sickle cell patients were investigated." Comment: The study was explicitly open label, so participants and personnel were not blinded. Because the intervention included oral NAC and outcomes included subjective/self-reported pain diary data and clinical events, knowledge of allocation could plausibly affect outcome reporting and care, making performance bias high.

Quote: "The primary end point of the study was reduction of erythrocyte phosphatidylserine (PS) expression as a direct indicator of erythrocyte membrane (oxidative) damage. Changes in markers of hemolysis (hemoglobin, reticulocytes, lactate dehydrogenase (LDH), and bilirubin) hypercoagulability, endothelial activation, and inflammation, and tolerability of oral NAC were secondary end points." and "Standard blood counts were performed in EDTA anti-coagulated blood ... LDH and total and direct bilirubin levels were measured ..." and "... erythrocyte PS expression was measured by flow cytometry ..." Comment: Outcomes were assessed using laboratory/automated measurements on blood samples rather than clinician or patient judgement, so knowledge of allocation is unlikely to have influenced outcome measurement. The outcomes reported in this study are objective biomarkers and counts, with no subjective patient-reported or clinician-rated outcome among the measured endpoints.

Quote: "Eleven patients ... were included in the study. One patient (P4) discontinued using NAC after 3 weeks and withdrew from the study. Two patients who used <80% of prescribed dosing are shown in gray in the figures." Comment: This is a very small study with 1/11 participants withdrawing (~9%), and an additional 2/11 with poor adherence (<80%). More importantly, the results table shows only n = 6 in the 1,200 mg group and n = 4 in the 2,400 mg group at follow-up, indicating that outcome data were not complete for all randomized participants and that analysed samples per arm were small. Although total withdrawal alone is below 30%, the attrition/exclusion pattern is not clearly and fully accounted for, and the study gives no robust missing-data analysis. With such limited numbers, missing data could plausibly bias the results.
